commands.review_summary:
The PR hardens the Codex app-server dynamic-tool bridge so unreadable fields, unsupported schemas, and Codex-invalid tool names are quarantined before registration while healthy sibling tools remain available.

PR surface: Source +130, Tests +115. Total +245 across 2 files.

Reproducibility: yes. from source inspection: current main directly reads dynamic-tool entries and fields before quarantine, and upstream Codex rejects invalid dynamic-tool names during thread start. I did not run a live failing assistant turn because this review is read-only.
commands.review_followup: - No ClawSweeper repair lane is needed because the refreshed patch addresses the prior identifier-validation blocker and remaining work is ordinary maintainer review plus CI gating.

target.title: fix(codex): quarantine unreadable dynamic tools
target.body:
## Summary

- Quarantines malformed Codex dynamic tools before app-server registration instead of letting one bad plugin tool poison the whole assistant turn.
- Reads and snapshots Codex-facing tool fields (`name`, `description`, `parameters`) before building specs, so throwing getters cannot crash later spec projection.
- Rejects Codex-invalid names before registration: empty names, leading/trailing whitespace, unsupported characters, over-128-character names, and reserved `mcp` / `mcp__*` names.
- Keeps healthy sibling tools available and executable while reporting the quarantined synthetic `fuzzplugin` tool in telemetry/diagnostics.
- Intentionally does not add broader doctor/plugin-SDK fuzzing here; this PR is the Codex app-server runtime slice only.

## Linked context

Related to the active tool-schema fuzzing/hardening work for unsupported runtime tool schemas crashing assistant turns before content.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: malformed dynamic tool entries, fields, schemas, and Codex-invalid names no longer crash Codex dynamic-tool registration before an assistant turn can produce content.
- Real environment tested: fresh OpenClaw `gwt` worktree from current `origin/main`; remote Linux AWS Crabbox changed gate.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/dynamic-tools.test.ts --reporter=dot`; `node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/codex/src/app-server/dynamic-tools.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/dynamic-tools.test.ts`; `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.extensions.json extensions/codex/src/app-server/dynamic-tools.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/dynamic-tools.test.ts`; `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base origin/main`; AWS Crabbox `pnpm check:changed` via `node scripts/crabbox-wrapper.mjs run --provider aws ...`.
- Evidence after fix: focused Codex dynamic-tool test passed 1 shard / 45 tests; targeted formatter and oxlint passed; diff checks passed; private reported-name scrub for touched Codex files passed; autoreview rerun was clean after fixing its invalid-name finding.
- Observed result after fix: unsupported-schema, unreadable-entry, unreadable-field, empty-name, whitespace-name, invalid-character, reserved-MCP-name, and too-long-name tools are quarantined while the healthy `message` tool remains registered.
- What was not tested: full live assistant turn with an externally installed hostile plugin; this PR uses synthetic `fuzzplugin` fixtures in the Codex app-server bridge.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: Azure Crabbox allocation timed out before command execution. AWS Crabbox ran `pnpm check:changed` with lanes `extensions` and `extensionTests`; extension and extension-test typechecks passed, but the gate failed later in unrelated current-main lint findings in `extensions/microsoft-foundry/provider.ts`.
- Before evidence: the previous bridge projected `tool.parameters`, `tool.name`, and `tool.description` directly while constructing Codex specs, so a malformed dynamic tool could throw or pass an invalid Codex name into the registration payload.

## Tests and validation

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/dynamic-tools.test.ts --reporter=dot` passed 1 shard / 45 tests.
- `node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/codex/src/app-server/dynamic-tools.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/dynamic-tools.test.ts` passed.
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.extensions.json extensions/codex/src/app-server/dynamic-tools.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/dynamic-tools.test.ts` passed.
- `git diff --check` and `git diff origin/main...HEAD --check` passed.
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base origin/main` passed after addressing one real invalid-name quarantine finding.
- AWS Crabbox `run_b87be20233c3`, lease `cbx_e585ff52c190`, provider `aws`, slug `crimson-crab`: remote `pnpm check:changed` selected `extensions` and `extensionTests`; all pre-lint guards and extension typecheck lanes passed; final result failed on unrelated current-main Microsoft Foundry lint.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes. Bad Codex dynamic tools are omitted/quarantined instead of being sent to Codex registration.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes. Tool execution availability changes for malformed dynamic tools: invalid tools are not callable, healthy siblings remain callable.

What is the highest-risk area?

Codex tool-name validation drift from the app-server contract.

How is that risk mitigated?

The bridge now enforces the Codex-facing identifier constraints before creating specs, and regressions cover the invalid-name classes that could still poison registration.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

Review the refreshed single-commit PR now based directly on `main`.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

CI needs to rerun on the refreshed head. Remote changed-gate proof is partial because current `origin/main` has unrelated Microsoft Foundry extension lint failures.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

Local autoreview found that non-empty invalid Codex names could still escape quarantine; this refresh adds explicit Codex identifier validation and focused regressions for those names.
target.author: vincentkoc
